question: Within which parts of a gastric gland are chief cells located

history.message:
Agent1 Round 1: The principal function of chief (peptic) cells is to secrete pepsinogen, an inactive precursor of the enzyme that breaks down proteins. These cells are typically found within the neck region of the gastric glands, where they extend into the surface epithelium from their basal location. This positioning allows them to be strategically placed near the opening of the gland so they can efficiently release pepsinogen into the stomach lumen upon stimulation by gastrin or other hormones.

Answer:

C: Neck

Note: The fundus and isthmus contain parietal cells; the gastric pits are openings through which glands enter the mucosa but not locations for cell localization.
Answer: C

The principal function of chief (peptic) cells is to secrete pepsinogen, an inactive precursor of the enzyme that breaks down proteins. Answer:

The principal function of chief (peptic) cells is to secrete pepsinogen, an inactive precursor of the enzyme that breaks down proteins. These cells are typically found within the neck region of the gastric glands, where they extend into the surface epithelium from their basal location. This positioning allows them to be strategically placed near the opening of the gland so they can efficiently release pepsinogen into the stomach lumen upon stimulation by gastrin or other hormones.
